turns.content: I'm so frustrated with how this works. I've spent hours trying to understand why the compiler treats a rarely called error handler differently from a hot path, even when both look like regular functions. It feels arbitrary until you explain the optimization logic behind it.

The friction comes from Rust's design philosophy regarding compile-time predictability versus runtime performance. When you mark a function with #[cold], you are explicitly telling LLVM that this code will be executed infrequently, allowing the optimizer to skip inlining or aggressive dead code elimination on those paths because it knows they won't impact the critical path. This interacts directly with #[inline(never)] which prevents the function from being called inline at all; if a function is both #[cold] and #[inline(never)], LLVM treats it as a guaranteed non-inliner that can be aggressively optimized out or moved to separate compilation units. For branch frequency hints, Rust uses std::intrinsics::likely and std::intrinsics::unlikely on nightly builds to provide metadata about the probability of true/false branches; these intrinsics help LLVM's branch predictor model make better decisions during code generation rather than just static analysis. In practice, this matters most in error paths where you want fast success but optimized failure, so marking those exit points as cold helps the compiler prioritize the common success path for immediate execution.
